title: Productive Collaboration and the Value of Diversity
obj: Students will be able to collaborate productively on team projects and explain why diverse perspectives make teams stronger.
content: Team roles and rotation
every team needs facilitation, note-taking, and building; rotate so everyone practices each
Norms of collaboration
listen to understand, build on others' ideas, disagree with the idea not the person, finish your commitments
Why diversity helps
different backgrounds catch different blind spots — especially important for AI teams (bias detection)
Conflict handling
surface disagreements early; restate the other person's view before rebutting; focus on shared goals
Remote and async
clear written updates, recorded decisions, documented handoffs — harder but possible
Leadership without authority
influence through preparation, asking good questions, helping others succeed
assess: Role-play: 3-person team disagrees on project direction; practice one conflict-handling technique
Reflect: describe a team experience where diversity caught a problem you wouldn't have
Short answer: how does a lack of diversity on an AI team translate into biased AI?

title: Computational Thinking Skills
obj: Students will be able to apply the four pillars of computational thinking to break down complex problems.
content: Decomposition
split a big problem into smaller sub-problems that can be solved independently
Pattern recognition
notice similarities across problems; reuse solutions from similar problems already solved
Abstraction
identify the essential features; ignore irrelevant details; focus on what matters for the solution
Algorithms
design a step-by-step process that solves the problem reliably
CT in AI
AI design uses all four: decompose a big AI system, recognize patterns in data, abstract the key features, design ML algorithm
Beyond coding
CT applies to cooking, music, sports strategy — anywhere you need to solve problems systematically
assess: Apply: use all 4 pillars to plan a homework-reminder app
Non-code example: apply CT to planning a school event
Short answer: why is abstraction critical when working with large datasets?

title: The Design Thinking Process
obj: Students will be able to explain the five phases of Design Thinking and apply them to a real-world problem.
content: Empathize
interview users, observe behavior, walk in their shoes; goal is understanding not solving
Define
synthesize findings into a clear problem statement: '(user) needs (need) because (insight)'
Ideate
brainstorm broadly without judgment; quantity before quality; then converge on the top 3
Prototype
build a rough, cheap version to test the idea — paper, clickable mockup, or MVP
Test
put the prototype in front of real users; observe; gather feedback; iterate
Iterative, not linear
you loop back — testing often sends you back to redefine the problem
assess: Lab: apply the 5 phases to 'improve how students check homework assignments' — produce 5 artifacts
Critique: given a failed product, identify which DT phase was skipped
Short answer: why do designers say 'fall in love with the problem, not the solution'?

title: Identifying Real-World Problems AI Can Solve
obj: Students will be able to identify real community problems that AI is well-suited to address.
content: Start with pain points
what frustrates people around you? long wait times, repeated questions, sorting work, forecasting
Check AI fit criteria
pattern recognition on data, repetitive judgment, or scale beyond human capacity
Constraint check
is data available? is the harm of errors acceptable? do stakeholders consent?
Community lens
problems in your school, neighborhood, family life — you already understand the context
Scoping the problem
'predict exact traffic' is too big; 'estimate cafeteria line length at lunch' is tractable
Check prior art
search for existing solutions first; don't reinvent something that already works
assess: Brainstorm: list 5 problems in your school that might benefit from AI
Scope: pick one and narrow it to a 4-week project
Short answer: why is narrowing scope a survival skill for student AI projects?

title: Collaboratively Designing an AI Solution
obj: Students will be able to work in a team to design an AI solution using Design Thinking and iterate based on feedback.
content: Divide by skill
each teammate takes a role (data, modeling, UX, presentation) but shares understanding across all
Empathize as a team
interview users together — different ears catch different things
Ideate broadly
many ideas first; pick the one that best balances impact + feasibility + team strengths
Design the AI component
what specifically does the AI do, with what input and output? where does it fit in the user flow?
Fast prototype
wireframe the app; fake the AI output with hardcoded examples to test user flow first
Design reviews
schedule 2 checkpoints with stakeholders; don't wait until the end for feedback
assess: Lab: in a 3-person team, apply this process to an identified problem; produce a design doc
Present: 5-minute design review; get feedback from another team
Short answer: why prototype the user flow with fake AI output before building the real model?

title: Prototyping and Building an AI Solution
obj: Students will be able to build a working prototype of an AI solution using appropriate tools.
content: Minimum viable prototype
what's the smallest version that proves the concept works? cut scope ruthlessly
No-code prototype first
use Teachable Machine, Lobe, or similar to validate the AI idea before writing code
Code prototype
then wrap in a simple Streamlit or Gradio interface for user testing
Tool choices
Streamlit for quick dashboards; Gradio for ML demos; Flask for full web apps
Model serving
for class projects, CPU inference is fine; only reach for GPU when latency becomes a blocker
Demo video
a 2-minute screen recording is more persuasive than 20 pages of docs
assess: Lab: build a Streamlit or Gradio app wrapping a trained model (use Teachable Machine output)
Record: 2-minute demo video with voiceover
Short answer: why is 'launch an ugly thing' better than 'plan a beautiful thing you never ship'?

title: Embedded Systems and Sensors for AI
obj: Students will be able to identify components of an embedded system and describe how sensors enable AI at the edge.
content: Microcontroller
tiny computer on a single chip; Arduino, micro:bit, ESP32; runs one program; low power
Microcomputer
full computer at the edge; Raspberry Pi; runs Linux; can run Python and light ML models
Common sensors
temperature, humidity, light, motion (PIR), distance (ultrasonic), camera, microphone, accelerometer
Actuators
motors, servos, LEDs, relays — what the embedded system uses to affect the physical world
Circuits basics
power + ground, breadboards, resistors, jumper wires; read a basic schematic
Edge AI
running ML models on the device itself; TinyML, TFLite Micro; keeps data local, no cloud needed
assess: Match: given 5 scenarios (smart thermostat, step counter, smart camera), pick sensors and microcontroller
Lab: identify the 4 main components on a Raspberry Pi or Arduino board
Short answer: when would you choose edge AI over cloud AI?

title: Building an Embedded Robot
obj: Students will be able to assemble a simple embedded robot or device that uses sensors and actuators for a specific task.
content: Plan the build
sketch the robot; list required parts (microcontroller, sensors, motors, chassis, battery)
Safety first
static discharge precautions; polarity when connecting; never short power to ground
Wiring
use a breadboard for prototyping; jumper wires to microcontroller pins; check schematic twice before powering on
Testing components individually
blink an LED; read a sensor value; drive a motor — verify each works before combining
Chassis and mounting
3D-print or laser-cut a chassis; secure components so they don't shake loose
Iterate
build in stages: locomotion works → add sensor → add AI decision → add output
assess: Lab: assemble a Raspberry Pi or Arduino with one sensor and one output (LED or buzzer); verify it powers on
Document: produce a wiring diagram with component list
Troubleshoot: given a robot that powers on but motors don't spin, describe your diagnostic steps

title: Programming a Sensor-Driven Robot
obj: Students will be able to write a program for an embedded robot that reads sensor input, makes decisions, and controls actuators.
content: Read sensor values
GPIO on Raspberry Pi or analog/digital pin on Arduino; poll in a loop or on interrupt
Decision logic
thresholding (if distance < 20 then stop); state machines for multi-step behaviors
Control actuators
digitalWrite for on/off; PWM for motor speed; servo libraries for angle control
The sense-plan-act loop
read sensors → update state → decide action → actuate → repeat (classic robotics architecture)
Integrate a small ML model
on Raspberry Pi: use TFLite to classify camera frames; on Arduino: use TinyML for keyword/gesture
Fail safely
what does the robot do if a sensor reading is invalid? default to stop, don't default to 'full speed'
assess: Lab: program a robot to stop when an object is closer than 20cm (ultrasonic sensor)
Extend: add a second behavior (e.g., turn away after stopping)
Short answer: why is 'fail to stop' safer than 'fail to continue' for a moving robot?

title: Debugging Hardware Issues
obj: Students will be able to apply a systematic debugging methodology to hardware problems.
content: Reproduce the problem
can you make it happen on demand? intermittent issues are the hardest
Narrow to component
swap one part at a time (known-good cable, fresh battery, different sensor)
Multimeter basics
measure continuity (cable intact?), voltage (power supplied?), resistance (component intact?)
Read error messages
serial console output is your friend; print sensor values and state to trace behavior
Check the obvious first
is it powered on? is the cable plugged in? is the code uploaded? is the battery charged?
When stuck, explain out loud
'rubber ducking' on hardware works too — you'll often spot the issue yourself
assess: Lab: intentionally introduce 3 faults in a working setup; have a partner debug them
Create a checklist: the 10 things you always check first when hardware misbehaves
Short answer: why is 'check the obvious first' the hardest step to remember?
